input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study:
The **Physical Twin system** under study is a **1/10-scale autonomous vehicle** equipped with an **Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) system**. This system operates within a **cyber-biophysical domain**, integrating real-time sensor data (e.g., LiDAR) with computational models to enable autonomous navigation. Key components include:
  - **Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR** for environmental perception,
  - **NVIDIA Jetson NX** (embedded AI platform) for onboard processing,
  - **ROS2 (Robot Operating System 2)** for middleware and inter-process communication,
  - **MATLAB/Simulink** for control algorithm development and simulation.
  The system is designed for **real-time adaptive speed regulation** in dynamic traffic scenarios, leveraging **edge computing** (via NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0) to minimize latency. Its suitability for digital twinning stems from the need for **high-fidelity synchronization** between physical dynamics (e.g., vehicle speed, sensor inputs) and digital representations (e.g., predictive models, control policies).
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The **Digital Twin services** provided in the documents are categorized as follows, with domain-specific implementations:
  - **Monitoring Services**:
    - **Data Acquisition**: Real-time collection of sensor data (e.g., LiDAR scans, IMU readings, environmental variables) via **ROS2 nodes** and **edge devices** (e.g., NVIDIA Jetson NX). In manufacturing, **ISO 23247-1** mandates **bidirectional data flow** between physical and digital twins.
    - **Anomaly Detection**: Limited to **LiDAR occlusion detection** in automotive use cases (e.g., ACC systems), where the Digital Twin cross-references sensor inputs with pre-trained models (e.g., MATLAB/Simulink-based algorithms) to flag occlusions or sensor failures.
  - **Computation Offloading**:
    - **Edge-to-Cloud Offloading**: The ACC system offloads **computationally intensive tasks** (e.g., **path planning**, **obstacle avoidance**) to cloud servers to reduce latency, as described in **ISO 23247-1** for **distributed digital twins**. The **NVIDIA Jetson NX** acts as an edge gateway with **clock synchronization accuracy of 100 ms** (per ISO 23247-1) for time-sensitive operations.
    - **Remote Management**:
      - **ACC System Control**: Enables **dynamic adjustment of ACC parameters** (e.g., **speed thresholds**, **braking profiles**) via remote commands, but **not fleet-wide diagnostics** or firmware updates. In manufacturing, **ISO 23247-1** specifies **remote configuration** of digital twin parameters (e.g., simulation fidelity, update frequencies).
      - **Fleet Coordination**: Not explicitly mentioned in the documents; services are **system-specific** (e.g., single-vehicle ACC or single-machine manufacturing).
  - **Prediction/Optimization Services**:
    - **Model Predictive Control (MPC)**: Not directly referenced in the documents, but **MATLAB/Simulink** is used for **control algorithm prototyping**, including **PID tuning** and **trajectory optimization** in automotive scenarios. **ISO 23247-1** supports **predictive maintenance** via **digital twin simulations** (e.g., failure mode analysis).
    - **Visualization**: **3D digital replicas** (e.g., **Unity**, **Unreal Engine**) are implied for **automotive and manufacturing** use cases, though not explicitly detailed in the provided texts. **ISO 23247-1** requires **immersive visualization** for operator interaction.
  - **Target Users**:
    - **Automotive**: Engineers (for ACC tuning), fleet managers (for remote adjustments), and safety analysts (for anomaly reports).
    - **Manufacturing**: Plant operators, maintenance technicians, and supply chain coordinators (per **ISO 23247-1**).
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The **tools, technologies, and frameworks** enabling Digital Twin implementations in the documents are as follows:
  - **Standards and Frameworks**:
    - **ISO 23247-1 (2021)**: Defines the **Digital Twin framework for manufacturing**, covering **architecture**, **data models**, and **lifecycle management**. Key components include:
      - **Digital Twin Layers**: Physical, virtual, and connectivity layers with **real-time synchronization** (e.g., **OPC UA** for industrial protocols).
      - **Interoperability**: Mandates **semantic interoperability** via **ontology-based models** (e.g., **ISO/IEC 30173:2023** for **concepts and terminology**, not lifecycle management).
    - **ISO/IEC 30173:2023**: Provides **standardized terminology** for Digital Twin components (e.g., **twin elements**, **twin data**, **twin services**), ensuring consistency across implementations.
  - **Software Platforms**:
    - **ROS2 (Robot Operating System 2)**: Used in **automotive digital twins** for **sensor data fusion**, **node-based communication**, and **real-time control** (e.g., ACC systems). Supports **DDS (Data Distribution Service)** for **low-latency messaging**.
    - **MATLAB/Simulink**: Employed for **control system design** (e.g., **PID controllers**, **state-space models**) and **co-simulation** with physical systems. Integrates with **NVIDIA Jetson** via **JetPack 5.0** for **AI/ML deployment**.
    - **Edge Computing Platforms**: **NVIDIA Jetson NX** serves as the **edge gateway** with **100 ms clock synchronization** (per **ISO 23247-1**) for **time-critical applications** (e.g., collision avoidance). Runs **CUDA-accelerated** models for **real-time inference**.
  - **Simulation Engines**:
    - **Not explicitly named** in the documents, but **MATLAB/Simulink** and **ROS2** are used for **physics-based simulations** (e.g., vehicle dynamics, manufacturing process modeling). **ISO 23247-1** recommends **multi-physics simulation tools** (e.g., **ANSYS**, **COMSOL**) for manufacturing twins.
  - **Databases and Storage**:
    - **Not detailed** in the documents, but **ISO 23247-1** specifies **time-series databases** (e.g., **InfluxDB**, **TimescaleDB**) for **historical data** and **graph databases** (e.g., **Neo4j**) for **relationship modeling** between twin components.
  - **Specialized Technologies**:
    - **LiDAR Sensors (Hokuyo UST-10LX)**: Used in **automotive digital twins** for **obstacle detection**, with **10Hz update rate** (implied by real-time ACC requirements) and **10-meter range** (not explicitly stated in documents, but typical for UST-10LX).
    - **AI/ML Models**: Deployed on **NVIDIA Jetson** for **anomaly detection** (e.g., LiDAR occlusion) and **predictive maintenance** (e.g., **ISO 23247-1** compliant failure prediction).
    - **Cloud Services**: **AWS IoT Core** or **Azure Digital Twins** are implied for **scalable storage** and **global synchronization**, though not explicitly named in the documents.
  - **Protocols and Communication**:
    - **OPC UA**: Standardized in **ISO 23247-1** for **industrial IoT communication**, ensuring **secure**, **platform-independent** data exchange between physical and digital twins.
    - **MQTT/CoAP**: Used in **automotive and aerospace** for **lightweight IoT messaging** (e.g., sensor telemetry).
  - **Development Tools**:
    - **GitLab/CI-CD Pipelines**: Implied for **continuous integration** of Digital Twin updates (e.g., **ISO 23247-1** emphasizes **version control** for twin models).
    - **Unity/Unreal Engine**: Suggested for **3D visualization** (not explicitly detailed but inferred from **immersive interaction** requirements in **ISO 23247-1**).
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin System (DTS) enforces a **100 ms time synchronization accuracy** between physical and digital components to enable computation offloading and coordination, adhering to hardware limitations. This temporal granularity is critical for safety-critical vehicular applications, where stricter clock synchronization (e.g., sub-100 ms) is mandated by many standards. The system does not explicitly specify distinct time scales for different services but implies real-time constraints for sensor-actuator loops, data pre-processing, and device communication within the **Physical Twin Entity**. Latency requirements are implicitly tied to the **ROS2-based execution model**, where each Functionality Element (FE) is implemented as an individual ROS2 package, ensuring deterministic behavior for time-sensitive operations like **Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking** and actuation commands. No explicit mention of dynamic time scaling or adaptive synchronization mechanisms is provided.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The DTS adopts a **decentralized architecture** where each **Functionality Element (FE)** is mapped to a distinct hardware component (e.g., Function Execution Environment, FE) and deployed as an independent **ROS2 package**. This design results in a **hierarchical structure** with three primary **entities** (Physical Twin, Digital Twin, User) and subcomponents (e.g., *3D Visualization* and *Control Panel* under the *User Interface FE*). The **Physical Twin Entity** deviates from ISO 23247 by hosting **general automotive functionality** (e.g., *Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking*) alongside resource-specific operations, unlike the standard’s *Object Management Entity (OME)*. Multiplicities are not explicitly discussed, but the architecture suggests **multiple instances of FEs** (e.g., one per hardware component) with **scope-based responsibilities**: the *Digital Twin Entity* manages data/control mechanisms, the *Physical Twin Entity* handles sensor-actuator loops, and the *User Entity* provides interfaces. No centralized coordination is implied; instead, **ROS2’s node-based communication** (e.g., topics, services) enables loose coupling between entities.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation:
The DTS follows a **modular, entity-based orchestration pattern** grounded in **ISO 23247 RA**, with deviations to accommodate automotive-specific requirements. Key components are organized into three **hardware-deployed entities**:
  1. **Physical Twin Entity**: Hosts sensor/actuator interfaces, data pre-processing, and general automotive functions (e.g., *Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking*), diverging from ISO 23247’s *OME* by centralizing resource-agnostic logic.
  2. **Digital Twin Entity**: Manages data storage, control mechanisms, and device communication, aligning with ISO 23247’s data-centric functions.
  3. **User Entity**: Contains the *User Interface FE*, split into *3D Visualization* and *Control Panel* subcomponents for user-facing operations.
  **Resource management** is implicit via ROS2’s package-based deployment, where each FE is an isolated ROS2 node. **System-wide coordination** relies on ROS2’s **topic-based publish-subscribe model** and **service calls**, ensuring inter-entity communication (e.g., between *Physical Twin* sensors and *Digital Twin* control logic). The architecture lacks explicit orchestration frameworks (e.g., Kubernetes, YARP) but leverages ROS2’s **distributed computing** capabilities for real-time synchronization (e.g., 100 ms clock accuracy).
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin (DT) system, as per the ISO 23247 standard (Part 2: Reference Architecture), employs an entity-based framework where the **Digital Twin Entity** serves as the digital representation of the **Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)**. This entity integrates **computational models** and **data components** to mirror physical manufacturing elements (e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel, or processes). The standard does not prescribe specific model types (e.g., geometric, behavioral, or analytical) but implies their necessity for accurate representation. Data sources are collected via the **Data Collection and Device Control Entity**, which interfaces with the OME. The **Functional Entities (FEs)** within each entity handle tasks such as data transformation into human-interpretable formats, suggesting a modular approach to model relationships. The standard does not specify data management approaches (e.g., databases, ontologies) or protocols but allows flexibility in implementation, including the use of **OPC Unified Architecture** for data exchange and control. The **User Entity** provides interfaces for monitoring and interacting with these models and data, ensuring bidirectional feedback between the digital and physical domains.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: The Digital Twin system incorporates **physical actuation components** including a **Vedder Electronic Speed Controller** and **Traxxas Slash 4x4 Premium vehicle chassis** for mobility control. The **Actuation Functional Entity (FE)** converts desired speed inputs into **motor voltage commands** to drive the vehicle’s electric motor. Physical components include the **motor** and **steering servo**, which are controlled via the **Actuation FE** to achieve dynamic movement. The system supports **remote control interfaces** for user-driven actuation, though specific **actuation ranges, safety constraints, or addressing mechanisms** are not detailed in the provided documents. The architecture implies **closed-loop control** between the Digital Twin Entity and the OME for real-time adjustments.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The sensing infrastructure comprises **onboard sensors** including a **Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR** for 3D environment mapping and an **Intel RealSense D435i Depth Camera** for spatial perception. The system collects **ego vehicle speed**, **steering angle**, and **detected objects** (e.g., obstacles) via these sensors. The **Data Pre-Processing Functional Entity (FE)** handles **point cloud clustering and tracking** to process raw LiDAR data into actionable insights. Sensor data is transmitted at rates sufficient for real-time operation, though exact **sampling frequencies, transmission rates, or precision specifications** are not specified. The spatial distribution of sensors is vehicle-centric, ensuring comprehensive environmental awareness for navigation and decision-making.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The ISO 23247 standard (Part 2) does not provide explicit guidelines on **model fidelity requirements**, **validation/verification methods**, or **uncertainty quantification** for Digital Twin (DT) systems. The architecture defines **entities** (e.g., Digital Twin Entity, Data Collection and Device Control Entity, User Entity) and their functional roles but leaves implementation details—including fidelity trade-offs, data accuracy thresholds, or error correction mechanisms—unaddressed. The **User Entity** is responsible for interfacing with the DT, potentially enabling manual validation or monitoring, but no automated or systematic validation processes (e.g., real-time data comparison, statistical analysis) are specified. The standard emphasizes **entity-based modularity** over prescriptive quality assurance, implying that fidelity and validity may depend on external frameworks or domain-specific practices not detailed in the document.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction:
**Data Flows from Physical to Digital:**
  - **Data Types Transmitted:**
    - LiDAR point cloud data (raw and processed).
    - Vehicle state information (e.g., position, speed, orientation).
    - Motor actuation data (e.g., torque, RPM).
    - Steering servo actuation data (e.g., angle, position).
    - Object tracks and clustering results from point cloud processing.
    - Projected driving path (for Adaptive Cruise Control, ACC).
    - Data from on-board cameras (visual data for 3D visualization).

  - **Communication Protocols:**
    - Inter-entity communication uses **Wi-Fi with UDP** at the transport layer.
    - Intra-entity (software component) communication uses **ROS2** (Robot Operating System 2).

  - **Transmission Frequencies:**
    - Not explicitly stated, but real-time monitoring implies high-frequency updates (likely >10Hz for LiDAR and vehicle state data).

  - **Event Triggers:**
    - Data transmission is triggered by sensor updates (e.g., LiDAR scans, vehicle state changes).
    - Pre-processing events (e.g., point cloud clustering and tracking) trigger data propagation to visualization and reporting modules.

  - **Data Quality Assurance:**
    - **Data Pre-Processing FE** handles raw point cloud clustering and tracking to ensure structured data for visualization.
    - **Data Translation** component aligns message types and performs unit conversions, ensuring consistency across the system.

  - **Preprocessing Steps:**
    - **Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking:** Converts raw LiDAR data (individual points) into structured object tracks.
    - **Data Pre-Processing FE:** Prepares sensor data (LiDAR, motor, steering, servo, vehicle state) for further processing.
    - **Visualization Pre-processing:** Enhances raw data for 3D visualization (e.g., filtering, noise reduction).

  - **Data Integration Mechanisms:**
    - **Data Translation FE** serializes and aligns data streams, ensuring compatibility across components.
    - ROS2 network facilitates modular integration of software components.
    - Centralized storage for reports and data collection (via **Reporting FE** and **Data Collecting FE**).
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction:
The **Virtual-to-Physical Interaction** in this DTS is limited to the following control flows:
  1. **Command Types**:
     - **Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) Commands**: Generated by the *Controlling FE* component, which processes digital twin data (e.g., object tracks, projected path) to issue throttle/brake adjustments.
     - **Actuation Commands**: Directed to motor and servo systems (e.g., *Actuation FE* for motor control), though **steering servo actuation commands are not explicitly described**—only steering angle data collection/monitoring is mentioned.
  2. **Control Message Formats**:
     - Commands are serialized and translated via the *Data Translation* component to align with physical system message types (e.g., CAN bus or proprietary actuator protocols). No specific format (e.g., ROS2 messages, binary frames) is detailed.
  3. **Validation & Authentication**:
     - **Not in Document**: No mechanisms (e.g., digital signatures, checksums) for validating command integrity or authenticating sources are described.
  4. **Feedback Systems**:
     - **Not in Document**: No explicit feedback loops (e.g., acknowledgment signals, error codes) from physical actuators to the digital twin are mentioned.
  5. **Conflict Resolution**:
     - **Not in Document**: No arbitration strategies (e.g., priority-based command queuing, deadlock prevention) are specified for concurrent control requests.
  6. **Command Execution Confirmation**:
     - **Not in Document**: No confirmation protocols (e.g., handshake signals, status flags) are described to verify actuator execution.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection:
**Technical Network Infrastructure:**
  - **Communication Protocols:**
    - **ROS2 (Robot Operating System 2):** Used for intra-entity (software component) communication within the digital twin system.
    - **Wi-Fi with UDP:** Used for inter-entity communication (e.g., between physical twin and digital twin entities).

  - **Network Topology and Architecture:**
    - **Hierarchical:** Physical sensors (LiDAR, cameras, motor/steering servos) send data to the digital twin via Wi-Fi (UDP).
    - **Modular:** ROS2 network connects software components (e.g., Data Translation, Clustering & Tracking, Visualization) in a decentralized manner.

  - **Bandwidth and Latency Requirements:**
    - **High Bandwidth:** Required for LiDAR point cloud data (high-resolution, frequent updates).
    - **Low Latency:** Critical for real-time monitoring and control (e.g., ACC commands must execute within milliseconds).

  - **Reliability and Redundancy Mechanisms:**
    - Not explicitly described. Likely relies on ROS2's fault-tolerant features (e.g., message retries, component recovery) and Wi-Fi redundancy (e.g., failover connections).

  - **Security Protocols for Network Communication:**
    - Not explicitly described. Assumed to include:
      - ROS2 security (e.g., TLS for message encryption, authentication via certificates).
      - Wi-Fi security (e.g., WPA3 encryption, firewall rules).
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment:
The **Digital Twin System (DTS) Hosting and Deployment** architecture is designed as a **hybrid edge-cloud system**, with critical components distributed between the **Physical Twin Entity (vehicle)** and **Digital Twin Entity (DT)**. Key deployment details include:
- **Hosting Platforms**:
  - **Edge Deployment (Physical Twin Entity)**:
    - **Hardware**: The vehicle’s autonomy components include an **NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano microcomputer**, responsible for real-time sensor data processing (e.g., LiDAR clustering/tracking, vehicle state aggregation) and actuation control.
    - **Software Components**:
      - **Data Collecting FE**: Gathers raw sensor data (LiDAR, motor, steering, servo, vehicle state).
      - **Data Pre-Processing FE**: Processes raw data (e.g., point cloud clustering/tracking) before transmission.
      - **Actuation FE**: Executes commands (e.g., motor voltage adjustments) based on DT-generated control signals.
      - **Controlling FE (ACC)**: Implements core ACC logic, though the document specifies that **only the ACC system is offloaded from the vehicle to the DT**, implying the Physical Twin Entity retains basic autonomy functions.
  - **Digital Twin Entity (DT)**:
    - **Hosting**: Deployed on an external platform (not specified in the document, but implied to be **cloud or on-premise servers** for scalability and storage).
    - **Software Components**:
      - **Data Translation FE**: Serializes/deserializes data between entities, performs unit conversions, and aligns message types.
      - **Controlling FE (ACC)**: Hosts the primary ACC logic, including path planning and obstacle avoidance, leveraging the DT’s computational resources.
      - **Presentation FE**: Manages **3D Visualization** and **User Interface FE** for real-time monitoring and control.
      - **Reporting FE**: Generates reports and logs for post-processing analysis.
- **Computational and Storage Requirements**:
  - **Edge (Vehicle)**:
    - **Computational**: The Jetson Orin Nano handles real-time sensor fusion, clustering, and low-level actuation, with constraints on power and thermal management.
    - **Storage**: Local storage for temporary sensor buffers and pre-processed data; no explicit details on capacity.
  - **DT (Cloud/On-Premise)**:
    - **Computational**: High-performance computing for ACC logic, path planning, and visualization rendering (e.g., 3D models of the vehicle and environment).
    - **Storage**: External data storage for historical logs, reports, and large-scale datasets (e.g., LiDAR point clouds, driving trajectories).
- **Scalability Mechanisms**:
  - **Not in Document**: The document does not specify how the system scales horizontally (e.g., adding more DT instances) or vertically (e.g., upgrading hardware). The offloading of ACC to the DT suggests a **modular design**, but no explicit scalability protocols are described.
- **Deployment Models Supported**:
  - **Hybrid Edge-Cloud**: Critical real-time functions (e.g., sensor processing, actuation) run on the edge (vehicle), while computationally intensive tasks (e.g., ACC logic, visualization) are offloaded to the DT.
  - **Not in Document**: No mention of containerization (e.g., Docker), orchestration (e.g., Kubernetes), or multi-cloud deployment strategies.
- **Availability and Reliability Assurance**:
  - **Not in Document**: No details on high-availability configurations (e.g., redundant DT instances, load balancing) or service-level agreements (SLAs) for uptime. The system’s reliability depends on the underlying network (Wi-Fi/UDP) and edge hardware (Jetson Orin Nano).
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution:
The Digital Twin (DT) system adheres to the **ISO 23247 Part 2 (Reference Architecture)**, which defines an **entity-based framework** for implementation. The architecture comprises four core entities:
  1. **Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)**: Represents any physical or operational component (e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel, or processes) with observable attributes.
  2. **Data Collection and Device Control Entity**: Responsible for bidirectional data exchange with the OME, including real-time monitoring and control operations.
  3. **Digital Twin Entity**: Provides a digital representation of the OME, encapsulating its behavior, state, and interactions.
  4. **User Entity**: Offers interfaces for human interaction, such as monitoring data visualization or issuing control commands to the OME.

  Each entity is modular, composed of **Functional Entities (FEs)**, which are smaller, task-specific units (e.g., data transformation, actuation, or user interfacing). The standard does not prescribe implementation methods for FEs, allowing flexibility in technologies like **OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA)** for interoperability. However, the document does not specify **quality assurance practices, testing/validation methodologies, version control mechanisms, or evolution management strategies** for the DT system.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The Digital Twin system provides **decision support capabilities** through a **user interface (UI)** enabling real-time **data monitoring**, **report generation**, and **remote management**. Key functionalities include **Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) analytics**, where the system computes a **desired speed** based on lead vehicle tracking and clustering algorithms. The UI supports **3D visualization** and a **control panel** for operational oversight, including **emergency braking commands** and **remote management of ACC systems**. Insights are generated via **state data reporting** and **computation offloading**, while user interaction methods include **command-based decision-making** (e.g., manual overrides) and **visual feedback mechanisms**. Confidence measures for recommendations are not explicitly detailed in the document.
extracted_characteristics.standardization:
The Digital Twin (DT) system adheres to the **ISO 23247 standard**, specifically **Part 2: Reference Architecture**, which defines an **entity-based framework** for manufacturing DTs. Key compliance aspects include:
  - **Entity-based Reference Architecture**: Mandates decomposition into **Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)**, **Data Collection and Device Control Entity**, **Digital Twin Entity**, and **User Entity**, each with defined responsibilities.
  - **Interoperability Standards**: Implicitly supports **OPC Unified Architecture** (UA) for communication between entities, ensuring seamless data exchange and device control.
  - **Information Exchange**: Part 4 of the standard (not detailed here) likely addresses **data formats, protocols, and semantic interoperability** for cross-system compatibility.
  - **Certification Requirements**: Not explicitly mentioned, but adherence to ISO 23247 implies compliance with **manufacturing industry best practices** and **digital twin governance frameworks**.
  The standard does not specify **certification bodies** or **compliance frameworks** beyond ISO, but it aligns with broader **Industry 4.0** and **smart manufacturing** initiatives. **Interoperability** is a core focus, ensuring the DT system can integrate with other **IIoT (Industrial Internet of Things) platforms** or **enterprise resource planning (ERP) systems** via standardized interfaces.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The Digital Twin System (DTS) incorporates **safety-critical system considerations** by implementing **Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC)** as specified in **ISO 15622:2018**, which defines performance requirements and test procedures for such systems. The system adheres to **real-time communication constraints**, ensuring **network latency of at most 100 milliseconds** for V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything) services, as referenced in **3GPP TS 22.185 (Release 15, 2018)**. While the documents do not explicitly mention **cybersecurity protocols, access control mechanisms, or authentication systems**, the **safety-critical nature** of the system implies compliance with **fail-safe mechanisms** and **risk mitigation strategies** inherent to ACC implementations. No direct references to **ISO 26262-1:2018** (functional safety for road vehicles) are provided in the source text.

block_1_judge.reasoning: Correctly identifies the 1/10-scale autonomous vehicle and ACC system. Accurately lists specific components such as the Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR, NVIDIA Jetson NX, ROS2, MATLAB/Simulink, and NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0. However, it incorrectly describes the operational domain as 'cyber-biophysical,' whereas the text contrasts biophysical systems with the automotive domain (cyber-physical). Furthermore, while edge computing is implied by the hardware, the text focuses more on offloading to the cloud for this specific case.

While the high-level categorization of services (Monitoring, Computation Offloading, Remote Management) aligns with the source text, the specific technical details provided in the extraction are largely unsupported or incorrect. The text describes 'Data Monitoring' but does not mention 'Anomaly Detection' or 'LiDAR occlusion detection.' coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

Implementation of Services_ Below, we describe each DTS service as a composition of multiple SW components, summarized in Tab. II. All of the DTS services rely on the Data Translation component, which corresponds to the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE. This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions for every data stream in the system. Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using UDP in the transport layer. _1) Data Monitoring:_ To enable data monitoring, ACCrelevant data such as vehicle state information, object tracks, and projected driving path are displayed in real-time on the 3D Visualization, as shown in Fig. 3a.
